questions.question: What is the main difference between a linked list and an array?
options: A) Linked lists are static data structures
B) Linked lists are dynamic data structures
C) Linked lists are used for sorting
D) Linked lists are used for searching
questions.explanation: Linked lists are dynamic data structures where each element points to the next element, whereas arrays are static data structures where elements are stored contiguously in memory.

questions.question: Which of the following operations can be performed on a linked list in O(1) time?
options: A) Insertion
B) Deletion
C) Traversal
D) All of the above
questions.explanation: Traversal operations such as printing or displaying the elements of a linked list can be performed in O(1) time because they do not require searching for any specific element.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the `next` pointer in a singly linked list?
options: A) To keep track of the current node
B) To store the previous node
C) To point to the next node in the list
D) To store the size of the list
questions.explanation: The `next` pointer is used to point to the next node in the linked list, allowing for efficient insertion and deletion of nodes.

questions.question: Which of the following operations is not possible on a self-referential linked list?
options: A) Deletion
B) Insertion
C) Traversal
D) Both deletion and insertion
questions.explanation: Self-referential linked lists do not have a `next` pointer, making deletion and insertion operations impossible.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the `head` pointer in a linked list?
options: A) To keep track of the current node
B) To store the previous node
C) To point to the first node in the list
D) To store the size of the list
questions.explanation: The `head` pointer is used to point to the first node in the linked list, providing a convenient way to access the beginning of the list.
